data.title: General Mills: I Was Early, But Reaffirm The Buy Amid Historically Low P/E
data.description:
The Tale Of My Two 2025 Long Picks

“Next to some other candidates, Google and General Mills are my headlining long-favorites for 2025.”

That was how I concluded my latest Buy-rated article about General Mills almost a year ago. Since then, Google is up 91%, while General Mills is down 25% even when factoring in dividends. While this top pick average of +33% versus the S&P 500’s +11% is still pleasing, let us now discuss whether parts of my GIS thesis were wrong, too early, or a combination of both.

Right, Wrong, Or Just Too Early?

Fully intact: Last December, I emphasized that I pick General Mills, among others, for portfolio-strategic reasons. It was “intended to provide a balance of uncorrelation to the overall market and a volatility hedge.” With raw beta factors at or below zero, this narrative is fully intact and valid for General Mills.

Just too early: Technology had already carried significant weight in my portfolio last year and continues to do so today, with overweight positions mainly in Alphabet and Microsoft, and an underweight position in Nvidia. High Alphabet conviction was also no secret here on Seeking Alpha, with the stock up 93%, 56% and 10% respectively since those articles. Positions like General Mills, on the other hand, were designed to balance these tech holdings. So far, those “balancing positions” have served me well on volatile days, helping the portfolio stay up even when tech-heavier indexes reflected panic in the last weeks. Nevertheless, GIS is down 25% over the past year and thus, I have been a bit too early on some consumer staples.

Flawed: My thesis appears to have been flawed in two aspects: 1) the assumption that real income growth should essentially end volume declines, and 2) applying this logic to General Mills. Yet nothing fundamentally impressive came out of General Mills’ earnings reports during the past few quarters. This leads us to the next chapter.

Topline Still Under Water

Except for one quarter, General Mills has not posted organic volume growth in at least three and a half years. Largely declining volumes over many quarters have now also started to weigh on margins. This is why management has already communicated a path back to volume growth as one of their top priorities. Pricing is no longer helping to grow organic volumes since FY24-Q1 (calendar year 2023-Q3). Before that, General Mills, like most consumer staples, benefitted disproportionately from the inflationary environment. Now, the opposite is the case, which is still a somewhat normal cycle observation. Nevertheless, I would definitely want to see a return back above the organic 0% line in the near future.

With its FY 2026 outlook, the company signals confidence in organic stabilization by guiding for flat organic sales. However, adjusted operating profits and EPS are expected to be down more than 10% amid a lower volume base. Free cash flow conversion will remain strong at over 95%. In the chart below, General Mills also visualized the cycle of consumer staples companies’ topline through inflationary environments that I explained earlier.

Strategic Positioning And Risks

As was long expected, General Mills just recently completed the divestiture of its US Yogurt business with gross proceeds of $1.8 billion. The “majority of net proceeds [will be] used to pay down debt,” according to the company.

General Mills now mainly comprises North America Retail [NAR] with a 58% revenue share and the highest operating profit margin within the company, equating to more than 20%. Unfortunately, it is exactly this segment that recently drove organic declines. With NAR, General Mills is especially exposed to the strength or weakness of the North American consumer. Add weight-loss drugs to the set of risks General Mills is confronted with. The company reports smaller portions among consumers after a current 12% and growing usage rate of those drugs among US adults. GIS responds by slowly shifting offerings and communication toward more health-conscious offerings.

The International segment contributes the second largest revenue share of 17%, but has the company’s lowest operating profit margins, recently only 9%. On the other hand, it is GIS’s bright spot in terms of growth, growing 4% organically during the most recent quarter. Anecdotally, I increasingly witness Nature Valley bars finding their way into our European supermarket shelves.

North America Foodservice (11% of revenue) remained stable in the last quarter. In connection with this segment, General Mills crowns itself the “undisputed leader in school breakfast.” Finally, Pet Food (14% of revenue) is arguably the company’s largest growth opportunity going forward, as there is not only market share to gain, but also the market itself is growing as a whole.

Dividend And Valuation

General Mills now has a starting dividend yield of 5.2%, significantly higher than last year’s 3.6% and thus even more notably above its own 10- and 5-year averages of around 3.5% and 3.4%, respectively. At the same time, 30-year TIPS yield 2.5%, not much more than last year, when they yielded 2.3%. With that, General Mills’ dividend has notably expanded its equity premium over TIPS yields to 2.7% (5.2% minus 2.5%), up from a 1.3% difference last year (3.6% minus 2.3%).

Furthermore, this dividend is still very well backed by free cash flow. The dividend payout ratio from free cash flow remains near historical levels of roughly 60%. Including share buybacks, on the other hand, total shareholder payouts have notably exceeded free cash flow recently. This likely prompts a reduction in the buyback pace, rendering the return to organic growth even more important.

The stock’s adjusted P/E of 12x on a TTM basis is now as low as it has only been in 2009 and 2019 over the last 20 years, when the average adjusted P/E was 16x. This might now somewhat limit the downside for the stock.

Takeaway

General Mills has disproportionately benefitted from high inflation until about two years ago and is struggling to stabilize volumes since disinflation adds pressure to the company’s top line. I would have expected stabilization to happen faster and now take management at its word when it expects a flat fiscal year 2026. Anything other than that would be disappointing. Under this assumption, however, General Mills now appears especially attractively valued near 20-year adjusted P/E lows and a historically high dividend yield of over 5%, which appears safe as well. Therefore, I stick to my Buy rating while acknowledging to have been somewhat early last year.

data.title: Don't Fear The Pivot: 2 BDCs Built For 10%+ Yields In New Rate Regime
data.description:
This year there have been two fundamental aspects that have depressed the BDCs (BIZD) and made investors nervous about their holdings, especially from the yield perspective.

First, we have transitioned away from a "higher-for-longer" talk, and instead now are really trying to guesstimate how many and how deep the next interest rate cuts will be.

Second, and this is not a new driver, but simply a pure fact: BDC earnings are strongly correlated to the changes in interest rates.

Granted, the interest rate and the corresponding BDC earnings sensitivities have not been the only variables in the BDC town that have caused some unfavorable repricing this year. For example, spread compression and rising fears of bankruptcies have done their thing as well. However, if we speak about the future and the overall direction of travel in the BDC earnings, then the former is no longer relevant (i.e., based on BDC Q3 earnings reports, the spreads have clearly stabilized), and the latter has really nothing to do with most of publicly traded BDCs, which carry relatively conservative underwriting policies (this includes having no exposures to non-revenue-generating/CapEx data center projects).

Now, going back to the interest rate aspect. This is a real problem and something that investors have to be cognizant of. For instance, in my recent piece titled - Don't Bury BDC Dividends Just Yet - I have integrated a table covering 15 most well-known BDCs and highlighting their NII per share sensitivities to various changes in the base rates.

On average, a 100 bps drop in the base rates leads to ~10% decline in annual NII per share generation. I think that the assumption of 100 bps decrease is quite fair and substantiated by the market's consensus expectations on the future level of interest rates (by the end of 2026).

So, if we fuse this essence with the items that I will list below, it would be only logical to conclude that quite many BDCs have their dividends under a big question mark:

  • The sector average base dividend coverage is exactly 100% - i.e., no margin of safety left at all.
  • Given that most BDCs have PIK income (non-cash item) between 5% to 10%, then we can say that the actual cash dividend coverage is, on average, already negative.
  • The sector average debt to equity is 1.19x, which means that BDCs, on average, do not have much gas left in tank to drive portfolio growth. It is obviously a problem given that there is no retained cash generation (i.e., everything is paid out). Equity issuance growth is not an option either because of the sector-wide discounts - i.e., sector average P/NAV is 0.83x.

I fully understand why quite many BDC investors have become skeptical. Investors, who seek durable passive income streams and want to avoid the stress associated with dividend cuts at all cost are, arguably, in worse position compared to those, who are willing and able to stomach some near to medium-term volatility, including absorbing income reductions.

Having said that, as it is usually the case everywhere in the investing world, there are solid exceptions to be found.

In the article below I will elaborate on two BDCs, which a) I personally hold in my portfolio and b) are well-positioned to avoid dividend cuts.

#1 - Trinity Capital, 14% yield

Trinity Capital Inc. (TRIN) is an internally managed BDCs, which invests primarily into senior secured first lien structure, while integrating equity co-investment clauses to capture equity-like upside potential.

The previous sentence sounds complex, but it really embodies some strong messages.

The fact that TRIN is internally managed is inherently positive to the P/NAV multiple as investors appreciate the shareholder value aligned focus and more friendly management cost base. P/NAV that is above 1.0x enables equity-driven growth, which is one of the most important fuels for BDC long term growth (just as for equity REITs since most of the internally generated cash is distributed to shareholders). Currently, TRIN has a P/NAV of 1.07x, which puts it among six publicly traded BDCs that have this luxury in place (very timely given the recovering M&A markets that finally allow BDCs to deploy capital at scale).

The focus to senior secured first lien loan investments implies that TRIN is exposed to the most defensive place in companies debt capital structures. In case there is a default, TRIN would be one of the first in line to seize the outstanding value. But, all in all, this is not so unique aspect as majority of BDCs have decided to play the game this way.

What is, however, a strong advantage for TRIN is its embedded equity-like exposure that stems from direct equity investments (and warrants) in pre-IPO stage companies. The value here lies in the fundamental fact that as the rates go down, equity valuation tend to go up. Since the M&A markets have started to gain momentum, the likelihood of TRIN realizing embedded equity stakes seems rather notable. Plus, if the Fed continues to cut the rates, the equity component will simply play an even bigger role, thereby offsetting the headwinds that might come from the SOFR-tied loan investments.

And speaking of interest rates, I have to say that TRIN is uniquely position here. Namely, TRIN has, according to my analysis of 15 largest / most well-known BDCs, the best profile when it comes to keeping the interest rate driven headwinds as low as possible. Namely, a 100 bps of a decline in SOFR would lead to only ~1.0% drop in the NII per share result. This is because of the bias towards fixed rate loan investments and, importantly, embedded interest rate floors for its loan investments.

Finally, TRIN's current base dividend coverage does not look that great - only at 102%. However, I have three things to say here.

First, TRIN's PIK stream is less than 2% of the total income, which is definitely of the best levels in the entire BDC space. The dividend is fully cash covered.

Second, TRIN has $409 million in off-balance sheet assets, which account for ~18% of the total asset base. The Management has communicated that it will utilize these unconsolidated joint ventures to drive earnings growth (i.e., originating loans at TRIN level and then selling then downstreaming them to JVs for minority income extraction without having to use leverage at holdco/TRIN level).

Third, and this is the most important element, TRIN will very likely continue to capitalize on its premium multiple to accommodate the massive pipeline in an accretive fashion, just as it has done so far this year. For example, in Q3, on a quarter-to-quarter basis, the NAV increased by ~8%. If we couple this with the equity realization potential, then I think that it would be fair to say that TRIN's base dividend coverage remains in solid shape (supported by growing NII and NAV base).

#2 - Fidus Investment, 11% yield

When it comes to Fidus Investment (FDUS) then, as opposed to TRIN, it does not have the luxury to fund growth through share issuances. FDUS is not an internally managed BDC and, currently, trades slightly below NAV (~3% discount to NAV).

Where FDUS has, however, a significant advantage is in the combination of the following two items:

  • Base dividend coverage surplus of 116%, which is, undoubtedly, way above the sector average and large enough to provide FDUS with the necessary fire power to grow the portfolio in a meaningful fashion by using internally generated equity.
  • Debt to equity of 0.7x, which ranks FDUS as the fifth least leveraged BDC in the entire sector. Given that the usual target capital structure range for BDCs is between 1.0x and 1.25x, we can easily conclude that FDUS has a lot of gas in the tank left to fuel portfolio expansion driven growth (i.e., growing "volumes" to offset "price" pressures from lower interest rates).

Based on these two variables alone, we can already make a substantiated conclusion that FDUS's base dividend is in a quite sustainable place.

What perhaps is not at evident is the underlying portfolio value that is associated with high-quality equity investments. The logic here is similar to that of TRIN, where the presence of equity stakes introduces a return factor that is negatively correlated to interest rate dynamics.

In FDUS's case the equity exposure is even more pronounced as for TRIN. The equity investment base accounts for ~12% of FDUS's total portfolio fair value, where these positions, as of Q3, 2025, had appreciated 154% over cost. What this positive unrealized appreciation means is that the positions are performing well and their valuations have gone up. We can see this by also looking at the portfolio-wide compy credit metrics: interest rate coverage of 3.1x and net leverage of 5.5x, which are one of the best statistics in the BDC game.

Again, if we put this in the context of strong transaction markets and very likely decreasing base rates from here, I think that it would be only reasonable to expect several equity monetization from FDUS in the foreseeable future. This would not only increase the NAV, but also directly support the dividend (and, potentially, provide a solid basis for distributing special dividends).

data.title: Reeves' Budget And U.S. Beige Book Still To Come After Market Swings Toward Fed Cut And PBOC Validates Stronger Yuan
data.description:
Overview

The US dollar (DXY) is mostly lower today. The pendulum has shifted hard in favor of a rate cut next month, and this has taken the wind from the dollar's sails. The New Zealand dollar is leading today's move after the central bank cut rates for perhaps the last time in the cycle. The Australian dollar also extended its gains on the back of firmer-than-expected October CPI. The other G10 currencies are posting minor gains ahead of the North American open but the Japanese yen, which is off about 0.3%. Most emerging market currencies are also firmer. The rise of the Chinese yuan to new highs for the year was reaffirmed by the PBOC, which lowered the dollar's reference rate for the fourth consecutive session. Although the US has several high-frequency data reports, the highlight is the Fed's Beige Book late in the North American session. And still to come is the UK's budget.

Equities are rallying today. All the large bourses in Asia-Pacific rallied. Indices in Japan (NKY:IND), Taiwan (TWSE), South Korea (KOSPI), and India rose by at least 1% today. The regional index is posting its third consecutive rise, as is Europe's STOXX 600 (STOXX), which is up about 0.40% through most of the European morning. US index futures (SPX) are modestly higher, set to extend their rallies. Benchmark 10-year yields jumped in Australia and New Zealand but are practically flat in Europe. The 10-year UK Gilt is unchanged, near 4.49%. The 10-year US Treasury yield (US10Y), which dipped below 4% yesterday for the first time this month, is slightly above there now. Gold is recouping yesterday's minor loss (~0.15%) and is up about 0.7% through the European morning. It stalled near $4170, an eight-day high. January WTI is in about a 30-cent range around $58. Yesterday, it recorded the low for the month, near $57.10. Last month's low was about $56.

USD: The Dollar Index consolidative phase is being challenged as the market re-prices a Fed cut next month to above 80% and with little time for Fed officials to push against it before the blackout period ahead of the FOMC meeting begins this weekend. Yesterday, in North America, it slipped to a four-day low near 99.65. It made a marginal new low today at 99.60. Nearby chart support may be around 99.50 and then 99.20. A bearish double-top pattern may be forming, but it takes a break of the 99.00 area to confirm. The US reports weekly initial jobless claims, September durable goods orders, but the market may be most sensitive to the headlines around the Beige Book. The absence of timely real sector data may give the anecdotal report more heft than usual. That said, Chair Powell has often referred to it. Surveys showed consumer sentiment is poor and household debt stress levels are high, as is the fear of losing one's job (five-year high). Still, Walmart (WMT) and Gap (GAP), for example, have reported strong quarterly earnings. Meanwhile, Boeing (BA) reported net new orders for 95 aircraft in September, the same as in August. Orders fell in October to 15. Orders jumped this month at the Dubai Air Show.

Euro: The euro reached a four-day high of about $1.1585 yesterday. It approached more formidable resistance today near $1.1600, which is reinforced by a little more than one billion euros of options at $1.1595 that expire today and another stack at $1.1575 that expires tomorrow at $1.1575. Even then, it probably takes a push above the mid-November highs around $1.1655 to suggest more than broad consolidation. The narrowing of the US two-year premium to a new low for the year near 145 bp is supportive for the single currencies. The eurozone reports money supply and lending figures tomorrow, and the highlight of the week is Friday's release of national November CPI figures from EMU's largest members.

CNY: The dollar was sold to new lows for the year against the offshore yuan yesterday, and has extended its losses today to almost CNH7.0750. It has not been lower since October 2024. Recall that it bottomed in late September last year near CNH6.9715. After reducing the dollar's reference rate for the third consecutive session yesterday, the greenback's heavier tone saw it extend the streak into the fourth session, matching the longest such streak since April-May. The fix was set at CNY7.0796, a new low since last October. The dollar has fallen to about CNY7.0780 today from a high near CNY7.12 last week and about CNY7.0845 yesterday, a new low since last November against the onshore yuan. The low last September was slightly above CNY7.0050. China reports October industrial profits tomorrow. They have improved for the past few months as the campaign against overinvestment takes hold. Some Chinese critics point to the low share of consumption as a proportion of GDP, even though consumption per capita is rising. As investment as a percentage of GDP falls, the share of consumption will rise as well as profitability.

JPY: As the US 10-year yield slipped to almost 4%, the dollar slipped to a new four-day low against the yen yesterday near JPY155.80. The JPY155.75 area holds the (50%) retracement of the last leg up that began from around JPY152.60 on November 14. The dollar is firmer today and is probing session highs near JPY156.50 late in the European morning. While in recent days the market has boosted the chances of a Fed cut next month, the odds of a BOJ hike have increased. At the end of last week, the swaps market was about 16%. It now stands near 40%. Japan reported that October PPI for services slowed to 2.7% in October from 3.1% in September. It peaked at 3.7% last year and has not been above 3.4% this year. Tomorrow, it will likely confirm the surge in machine tool orders of 16.8% year-over-year in October from 11.0% in September. It is the strongest since June 2022. Friday sees several important high-frequency data points that may include slightly softer Tokyo CPI (November), the unemployment rate ticking low (2.5% vs. 2.6%), firm retail sales (0.8%) and a fall in October industrial output after the 2.6% jump in September, the largest rise since March 2024.

GBP: Chancellor Reeves will present the budget today beginning around 7:30 AM ET. It has been highly anticipated, and some parts apparently leaked. Her task is to restore some fiscal headroom while not violating key campaign promises and/or providing more fodder for Farage's UK Reform Party that is running strong in polls. The Office for Budget Responsibility will provide updated economic projections. In addition to providing new fiscal space, Reeves is expected to unveil measures that could reduce inflation by around 0.4% by mid-2026 that would ostensibly give the central bank more scope to cut interest rates, which reduce debt servicing costs. Such measures may include freezing train fares, and freezing fuel duty for another year. Reports yesterday suggested that a tourist tax may be imposed by local officials of around GBP2 a night. It is possible that an exit charge could be levied on people moving abroad, and limited partnerships may face higher taxes. Sterling has pushed higher in the run-up to today's budget. It reached an eight-day high against the dollar yesterday near $1.3215 and settled above the 20-day moving average for the first time since October 17. Sterling is consolidating ahead of the budget announcement at a little more than a third of a cent below $1.3200. It posted its highest settlement close of the month above $1.3200. Sterling traded near a three-week high against the euro yesterday and is consolidating now before the budget.

CAD: Despite the US dollar's setback against the euro, yen, and sterling yesterday, it proved more resilient against the dollar bloc currencies and the Scandis. It set a range last Friday of roughly CAD1.4080-1.4130 and has remained in the range in the past couple of days but marginally took it out today, slipping to about CAD1.4070. The daily momentum indicators still look constructive. The week's data highlights still lie ahead: Q3 current account deficit (deteriorating this year) is due tomorrow, and September and Q3 GDP on Friday.

AUD: The Australian dollar continued to struggle to sustain upside momentum. It recovered from near a 3-month low near $0.6420 before the weekend but stalled around $0.6470 on Monday. After returning to almost $0.6435 yesterday, the Aussie had a running start and took out $0.6470 by a few hundredths of a cent to reach a three-day high. The firmer-than-expected CPI today lifted the Australian dollar a little above $0.6510 today. Nearby resistance is seen near $0.6520. Options for nearly A$1.2 bln at $0.6500 and A$1.7 bln at $0.6535 expire today. Australia reported firmer-than-expected headline CPI that rose to 3.8% from 3.6%, and the core (trimmed mean) rose to 3.3% from 3.2%. The futures market has already given up previous hopes of a rate cut next month. The highest the odds had begun this month were around 27% in the first week, and they are now less than 5%. The other notable development was that the Reserve Bank of New Zealand delivered another cut in the easing cycle that began in August 2024. The new target rate is 2.25%. It was a "hawkish cut", as the RBNZ did not project another cut. The market was leaning to see today's cut as probably the end of the cycle, and this reinforced it. The New Zealand dollar rallied from a seven-month low at the end of last week (~$0.5380) to almost $0.5700 today, retracing about half of this month's decline. The next retracement (61.8%) is near $0.5720. It has pulled back to about $0.5670, where options for almost NZD800 mln expire.

MXN: A cap to the dollar has been forged over the past three sessions around MXN18.53, which is around the (50%) retracement of the greenback's decline from the month's high near MXN18.77. The dollar recorded lower lows for the third consecutive session today. Monday's low was about MXN18.4150, and it was sold yesterday to almost MXN18.3730. Today's low is around MXN18.3580. Yesterday, Mexico reported flat September retail sales (vs. the median forecast in Bloomberg's survey of -0.4%). The year-over-year rate improved to 3.3% from 2.4%, which is the best since March. Separately, in Q3, Mexico's current account balance swung into surplus ($2.3 bln) after deficits in Q1 and Q2. Through Q3, Mexico reported a $10.63 bln deficit, compared with a deficit near $27.56 bln in the first three quarters of 2024. Note that through September, worker remittances alone account for $45.7 bln, keeping the external account in a healthy position. Tomorrow, Mexico's central bank releases its inflation report where it updates its economic forecasts.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Palantir's Sell-Off May Be Just The Beginning
data.description:
The Market Started To Realize Palantir's Fragility

I've been skeptical about Palantir's (PLTR) continued consensus-beating momentum in the past, and that was a reason for my most recent "Sell" rating confirmation in early September 2025. The firm actually beat the estimates for its Q3 2025 quite strongly - by 8.19% and 25.46% on revenue and EPS, respectively [Seeking Alpha data] - and it went against my bear case (for now). But what the CEO Alex Karp called "arguably the best results that any software company has ever delivered" didn't lead to PLTR's rally continuation. It's currently trading 25%+ off all-time highs, based on YCharts data:

The stock is only 10% away from its 200-day moving average, and the RSI technical indicator turned the lowest it's been since March/April 2025 - all that amid fundamental strength and rising earnings expectations after the firm's beating Q3 results:

Despite this seeming controversy, I'm not calling to buy the dip (and I'm not upgrading to "Hold" either). I believe that the sell-off we've been seeing since the beginning of November is indicating fragility in Palantir's bullish case, and the market may have started to realize that it's not willing to overpay for Palantir's strength, because there are other tech names in today's market with similar growth rates at cheaper multiples. The post-earnings correction looks like a process of Palantir growing out of its excessive valuation, and we still see no reasonableness on that front. Until we see it in reality, I think PLTR is likely to keep falling, and that's why I remain bearish on the stock.

Don't Underestimate The Risks To Palantir's Sales Cycle

I understand the narrative that we're in a supercycle of AI CAPEX, hardly compared to anything else in the past, and that Palantir is the purest enterprise AI platform, so it should enjoy superior business growth rates and its stock should have a premium valuation. The nuance here is how Palantir is growing and what that implies for its future. We saw in the firm's Q3 report about $2.8 billion of TCV, with U.S. commercial TCV being up >6x YoY on a dollar‑weighted duration basis. While it's a massive YoY growth in itself, it effectively means that PLTR's customers aren't just signing small pilots, but jumping straight into large multi‑year enterprise‑wide agreements. RBC analysts were pointing at PLTR's U.S. commercial Al contracts potentially pulling forward demand in the next years (see their recent update on PLTR's Q3). In my opinion, if PLTR's customer, which might have grown from $5 million to $50 million over 5 years, instead signs a $100 million 5‑year AIP deal today, the company's bookings should explode, but some of that growth is simply shifting from the future into the present. Their recognized revenue and backlog metrics rise and make current growth look exceptional, but a large part of what we're seeing is demand that likely would have materialized over 3-5 years being locked in upfront. I think all this can create a risk of an "AI digestion phase" that consensus numbers are not fully acknowledging.

As the AI tech develops and scales (and it's destined to be the case, given the massive capital inflows in the niche), shareholders start to ask C-suite executives questions on how their companies are implementing AI at the operational level. The executives are rushing to sign multi‑year AIP and Foundry deals with Palantir, which is compressing sales cycles. A meaningful slice of Palantir's highest‑spending customers will see their budgets already locked in, as I've already explained above, and then the CFOs will have to show sufficient ROIC to approve second‑wave deployments. And we're already seeing some negative signs that AI investments may not be as successful in terms of return on invested capital as they might have been viewed just a couple of years ago.

Across industries, investment in AI is rising fast. According to Deloitte’s 2025 survey, 85% of organisations increased their investment in the past 12 months, and 91% plan to increase it again this year. Yet despite this momentum, most respondents reported achieving satisfactory ROI on a typical AI use case within two to four years. This is significantly longer than the typical payback period of seven to 12 months expected for technology investments. Only 6% reported payback in under a year, and even among the most successful projects, just 13% saw returns within 12 months.

Source: Deloitte

HSBC analysts, who have a bullish rating on PLTR, noted in their research update some risks to the ROIC dynamics as well (although it's not specifically about PLTR, but still):

“We expect US commercial segment’s revenue to rise at a CAGR of 59.3% over 2025-29 to $9.3B in 2029,” analyst Stephen Bersey wrote. Bersey kept his Hold rating on Palantir and bumped his price target to $197 from $181 after the results.

“We would, however, flag that we are seeing several anecdotes and reports suggesting that the ROIC on internal AI projects that enterprises are experimenting with (not specific to Palantir) is often disappointing. This may slow the tempo of future contract signings, in our opinion,” Bersey noted.

Source: Seeking Alpha News, emphasis added by Oakoff

It's a major risk for PLTR because when those clients (and new prospective clients) may choose hyperscaler‑native AI stacks. As a result, I think that Palantir's effective serviceable market over the next few years is likely to grow more slowly than the current headline numbers imply, and if so, the current forward-looking earnings estimates are not properly reflecting the risks to Palantir's sales cycle that I've just described.

Plus, Palantir's bootcamps and FDE model are fantastic for starting AI programs quickly, but they don't seem to guarantee that every large customer will see adequate ROIC on a $50-200 million multi‑year commitment going forward. This is an execution risk, which is not priced in right now, as far as I can see it. But Wall Street seems to be valuing Palantir as if virtually all of this front‑loaded AI spend will renew, expand, and then trigger a long tail of high‑margin upsell:

The more anecdotes we hear from HSBC and their colleagues about disappointing AI ROIC ahead, the higher the risk that future large‑deal velocity slows (even if existing deployments work reasonably well). And what I see to date looks like a simple extrapolation of Palantir's current strengthening well into the next decade.

Another thing that I don't like seeing is that the CEO seems to be cultivating an ideological brand with his commentary (at least that's how it looks to me):

How did a company, which stood by the American Warfighter, marine special operators, people in clandestine services who stood up for a right of free speech and was really the first company to be completely anti-woke. How did this company stick up for the American Warfighter, actually give normal Americans venture quality results.

Source: Alex Karp's commentary on the latest earnings call, emphasis added

I have nothing against this view in particular, but it's a concerning sign to me that PLTR may become a target for opposite-minded politicians. I don't see investors discussing this much, but I view it as a hidden risk.

Palantir's Valuation Update

On the forward basis, PLTR is trading at 59x sales and 156x earnings, while on the FY2027 basis, these numbers are forecasted to compress to 43.56x and 110x, respectively. I still don't think that the level of multiple contractions is sufficient, even if Palantir's revenue and EPS will be growing at CAGRs of 31.9% and 30.76%, respectively, in the long term (9-year period, based on Seeking Alpha data).

There are cheaper alternatives on the market with similar growth rates ahead. For example, we see that PLTR's EPS is set to double from FY2025's $0.72 to $1.40 by the end of FY2027. SoFi Technology's (SOFI) EPS should grow by 124%+ over the same period, but its FY2027 P/E ratio is almost 3 times below that of Palantir (30x vs. 110x).

Even Nvidia's (NVDA) EPS is projected to almost double in the next 2 years, but its 2-year forward P/E ratio is only 18.4x, which is ~6 times lower than PLTR's.

I'd like to see PLTR's P/E ratio closer to 30-40 in 2 years from now, but even if we assume CrowdStrike's (CRWD) multiple of 79x, the downside in PLTR's stock price will amount to an additional 30%+.

But let's say that PLTR can beat the current FY2027 consensus estimate by 30% given the accelerated growth momentum that it's going through. In this case, we shall see $1.82 instead of today's consensus of $1.40 (FY2027). Putting a 70x earnings multiple on that figure, we'll have a price target of $127.4/share. This gives a downside potential of over 18% in the next years, and it keeps my bearish stance in place even after Palantir's drop of 25% in recent weeks.

Upside Risks

The main risk to my updated bearish thesis is that what I called "pull‑forward" demand would instead look like the first step of a much longer and stronger demand environment. As a result, Palantir could grow into more of its valuation if the earnings beating momentum stays at its current level of 15%-20%+. Another upside risk is that if Palantir can embed itself as a cloud‑agnostic coordination layer that everyone plugs into, rather than a point solution to be displaced by hyperscalers, its serviceable market and pricing power could be structurally higher than I'm assuming in my reasoning part. That's what can keep the valuation multiples elevated, and with the constant premium in place, PLTR can add hundreds of billions of dollars to its market cap as it keeps scaling.

Final Thoughts

While I concur that Palantir's business growth is accelerating rapidly, and there's no metric out there that we can point at in a negative sense, I think the recent 25%+ drawdown is less an overreaction to a single quarter and more a sign that the market has started to reassess how much it is willing to pay for Palantir's very real strengths. At ~59x forward sales and ~156x forward earnings, compressing only to ~44x sales and ~110x earnings by FY2027 on consensus, investors are paying a substantial premium versus other high‑growth tech peers with similar growth rates, and I think it leaves a lot of room for further valuation contraction ahead as the currently hidden risks start to be visible. I see an 18%+ downside in the next 2 years, hence rating PLTR as a "Sell".

Good luck with your investments!

data.title: The Most Important Development In Yesterday's Session
data.description:
Stocks climbed for a third day to carve out what looks like a bottom in the broad market over the past month, but something stood out in yesterday’s session that I think marks a turning point in this bull market. For the past two years we have been at the mercy of market bellwether Nvidia (NVDA), which is the largest company in the world commanding a weighting of 8% in the S&P 500. Yesterday, the stock was the second worst performer in the index, closing down 2.6%. Its underperformance is due to new competition from one of its largest customers. Alphabet’s (GOOG) tensor processing units (TPUs) are presenting stiff competition for Nvidia’s AI chips in the race to build out AI data centers. Normally, the decline in Nvidia’s stock price would have taken the rest of the market down with it, but not yesterday.

Yesterday, investors bought everything but Nvidia in a broadening of performance we have not seen in some time. The second most important development was that the more domestically focused Russell 2000 index was the best performer, rising 2.2% and regaining its short-term moving average in a technical breakout. The S&P 500 index and the Nasdaq Composite also rose above their 50-day moving averages. The year-end rally is well underway.

Producer prices lead consumer prices, and yesterday’s delayed Producer Price Index (PPI) report for September showed that core prices, which exclude food and energy, rose just 0.1%. That results in a year-over-year increase of just 2.6%, which is the lowest annualized rate in the past year. Services prices were unchanged after falling 0.3% the month before. They account for two thirds of the index and have been a focal point of the Fed, which should keep Fed officials leaning towards a rate cut in two weeks.

Retail sales rose a more tepid 0.2% in September, which is not surprising given the robust 0.6% gains in the prior two months. That results in year-over-year growth of 4.3%, which is still a healthy rate of spending, indicating consumers may be dismayed by their view of the economic outlook, but they continue to spend at a healthy rate. Notable is that spending at bars and restaurants rose 0.7% during the month, which is the most discretionary of all the categories. If consumers were acting the way they say they feel, this would be the first category to taper off dramatically. It has not.

News that President Trump will announce his new Fed Chairman next month with his selection down to five candidates has investors recognizing that no matter who he picks it will be someone far more dovish than Chairman Powell. The new Fed Chair, who I think is likely to be Keven Hassett, will increase the chances that we see a more rapid decline in short-term interest rates next year. I see the rate falling to at least 3%, if not lower, during the first half of next year. This should be an additional tailwind to risk asset prices during the first half of 2026.

Enjoy your Thanksgiving holiday!

data.title: How Can New Hiring And The Unemployment Rate Rise At The Same Time?
data.description:
By Kevin Flanagan

Well, it certainly did take a while, but the Fed and the markets finally got a jobs report. Alas, it was for the month of September, so in a sense, not exactly current news, but still, some “fresh” insights, nevertheless. The underlying data did cause some confusion for investors, though, as both new job creation (nonfarm payrolls) and the unemployment rate rose in the same month. That begged the question: how can that happen?

How Can That Happen?

In order to understand the jobs data a little better, you need to know that the Bureau of Labor Statistics (BLS) conducts two separate surveys for each monthly report: the household survey and the establishment survey.

  • The household survey gives us the civilian labor force, the labor force participation rate, and, of course, the unemployment rate. Interestingly, it also includes its own measure of new jobs, civilian employment.
  • The establishment survey consists of nonfarm payrolls, hours worked and average hourly earnings data.

Since nonfarm payrolls and the unemployment rate come from two different surveys, at times they can move in counterintuitive directions on a monthly basis, but over time, this anomaly is corrected.

Jobs Report Review

  • Total nonfarm payrolls rose by more than double the consensus estimate, coming in with a gain of +119k. This resulted in the three-month moving average rising to +62k (the highest since May) vs. the prior month’s reading of +18k.
  • The unemployment rate rose by +0.1 pp to 4.4%, which created some market anxiety.

However, the increase in the jobless rate was essentially due to a surge in the labor force, not necessarily layoffs. Without the increase in the labor force participation rate, the unemployment rate would have been unchanged, if not lower.

Conclusion

In terms of upcoming post-shutdown data of consequence, this was the last full Employment Situation report before the next FOMC meeting on December 10. In addition, since the BLS canceled the October Consumer Price Index (CPI) report, there won’t be another CPI release until December 18, once again after the aforementioned Fed gathering. Recent Fed-speak and the October FOMC minutes underscore a Fed “divided,” with a good number of regional bank presidents wanting to pause rate cuts while New York Fed President Williams and Governor Waller still seem to favor another easing move. The September jobs numbers and lack of additional “full” employment and inflation data until after the FOMC meeting play into the “pause” argument, but it will be a ‘close call’ with another cut still on the table.

Kevin Flanagan, Head of Fixed Income Strategy

As part of WisdomTree’s Investment Strategy group, Kevin serves as Head of Fixed Income Strategy. In this role, he contributes to the asset allocation team, writes fixed income-related content and travels with the sales team, conducting client-facing meetings and providing expertise on WisdomTree’s existing and future bond ETFs. In addition, Kevin works closely with the fixed income team. Prior to joining WisdomTree, Kevin spent 30 years at Morgan Stanley, where he was Managing Director and Chief Fixed Income Strategist for Wealth Management. He was responsible for tactical and strategic recommendations and created asset allocation models for fixed income securities. He was a contributor to the Morgan Stanley Wealth Management Global Investment Committee, primary author of Morgan Stanley Wealth Management’s monthly and weekly fixed income publications, and collaborated with the firm’s Research and Consulting Group Divisions to build ETF and fund manager asset allocation models. Kevin has an MBA from Pace University’s Lubin Graduate School of Business, and a B.S in Finance from Fairfield University.

Original Post

data.title: BINC: Strong, Well-Rounded Income ETF, Good Quality, Dividends, Performance And Risk
data.description:
BINC - Investment Thesis

The iShares Flexible Income Active ETF (BINC) is one of my top income ETFs, due to its strong, and simple, investment thesis. BINC's investment thesis rests on its:

  • Diversified portfolio, with exposure to most fixed-income sub-asset classes
  • Below-average risk and volatility, across quantitative and qualitative metrics
  • Above-average dividend yield and performance track-record, with a 5.2% yield, and consistent outperformance relative to most bonds
  • Strong risk-adjusted returns, across metrics

As should be clear from the above, BINC is an incredibly strong, well-rounded investment, and one without significant risks to boot. Let's have a closer look at each of the points above.

Diversified Portfolio

BINC is an actively-managed bond ETF, with exposure to most fixed-income sub-asset classes, including several niche ones. BINC invests in corporate bonds, both U.S. and foreign, both investment-grade and high-yield. It also invests in MBS, agency and non-agency, residential and commercial. It includes more niche investments too, such as CLOs, ABS, and emerging market bonds. It does not currently invest in treasuries, but MBS are something of a replacement for these. Asset allocations are as follows:

BINC's portfolio is incredibly well-diversified, much more so than most of its peers. It is arguably more diversified than the largest bond ETFs too. The Vanguard Total Bond Market Index Fund ETF (BND), for instance, does not invest in high-yield bonds, CLOs, or ABS, and neither do most of the larger bond ETFs. BINC does, making it a much more diversified fund than most.

BINC's diversified portfolio reduces risk, volatility, and the potential for significant losses or underperformance due to issues in any one specific fixed-income sector. As an example, the fund shouldn't suffer significant losses from credit spreads widening, due to its significant investments in quality MBS, and similar assets. It shouldn't suffer significant losses from a rise in rates either, due to its investments in CLOs, ABS, and shorter-term bonds. Lots of scenarios or events could cause BINC to experience some amount of losses, but significant, outsized losses or underperformance seem unlikely, considering the fund's diversification.

Below-Average Risk and Volatility

BINC is a comparatively safe, low-risk, low-volatility investment across quantitative and qualitative metrics.

Credit quality is reasonably good, with the fund focusing on investment-grade securities, especially those rated AAA. It holds sizable investments in riskier bonds though, with these accounting for just over 1/3rd of its portfolio.

Rate risk is below-average, with a duration of 4.2 years, compared to a bond average of 5.8, as per BND.

Realized volatility has been below-average too, albeit with a sizable spike earlier in the year.

Technically drawdowns are below-average too, but with inception in mid-2023, the fund has yet to experience an actual recession or bear market. It did suffer smaller losses earlier in the year, as markets tumbled due to the imposition of tariffs.

On more qualitative metrics, BINC's diversified portfolio reduces risk, volatility, and drawdowns too. As mentioned previously, it also significantly reduces the probability of significant losses or underperformance due to issues in any one specific sub-asset class.

In my opinion, these last considerations are of incredible importance, albeit not easy to quantify. At the same time, BINC compares quite favorably to most of its peers in these issues. CLOs, for instance, have below-average volatility too:

But because CLO ETFs only invest in CLOs, their dividends should plummet if the Fed slashes rates, which should all but ensure their underperformance. BINC's CLO investments would react in much the same way, but its other investments would not, reducing the (negative) impact of any cuts. Lots of high-quality ETFs have below-average risk and volatility, but BINC's diversification is rarer, and a significant benefit for shareholders.

BINC does not significantly differ from its benchmark, so returns shouldn't significantly deviate from the same, which is something of a reduction in risk too. As an example, as the fund does have some duration, it should see some gains during periods of declining rates, minimizing any potential underperformance during a period of declining rates. Compare BINC with BBB CLOs, with have negligible duration, YTD, for instance.

BINC's returns have been much closer to its benchmark compared to CLOs YTD, and the same should be true if rates continue to decline. Although the fund does have many differences relative to its benchmark, none seem sizable enough to plausibly cause significant underperformance moving forward.

Above-Average Dividend Yield and Performance Track-Record

BINC currently yields 5.2%, excluding an abnormally large dividend late last year. It is a reasonably good yield on an absolute basis, and higher than that of most bonds and bond sub-asset classes.

More broadly, interest rates remain elevated, as do rates on most fixed-income bond sub-asset classes. Bonds are attractive right now, benefitting bond ETFs including BINC.

BINC's above-average dividend yield should boost returns moving forward and should lead to long-term outperformance. Other factors impact a fund's returns too, so outperformance is not guaranteed, but it is definitely likelier because of the dividends, likelier than not.

BINC has outperformed most bonds and bond sub-asset classes since inception, as expected. It has trailed high-yield corporate bonds, although these have significantly higher credit risk.

As BINC leans short-term, thought to compare it with shorter-term bond benchmarks too. The fund has outperformed investment-grade bond indexes, underperformed riskier high-yield bonds, same as above.

BINC's performance looks quite strong considering the fund's credit quality. BINC is around 2/3rds high-quality investment-grade bonds, 1/3rd riskier securities, but its returns are closer to the latter. So, quality leans investment-grade, returns lean high-yield. Risk-adjusted returns should then be quite strong, as seems to be the case looking at Sharpe and Sortino ratios.

Conclusion - Strong, Well-Rounded Investment

I previously described BINC as being a well-rounded investment, and one without significant risks. Reading through the article, I think this is quite evident.

BINC has reasonably good credit quality, below-average duration, below-average realized volatility and drawdowns, above-average dividends and returns. Due to its diversification and characteristics, significant losses or underperformance seems unlikely. Lots of benefits, few downsides, no significant issues or risks. BINC is thus a strong investment opportunity, and a buy.

data.title: SoFi Technologies: The Margin Of Safety Is Insufficient To Avoid A Downgrade
data.description:
One of my best calls over the last few years now has been SoFi Technologies (SOFI), a financial technologies company that has achieved rapid growth. Since my last article about the business, an article where I rated it a ‘buy’ back in August of this year, shares have appreciated 14.7%. That dwarfs the 3.5% rise that the S&P 500 has seen over the same window of time. But that's nothing. Since my first ‘buy’ rated article on the company was published in December of 2023, nearly two years ago, the stock has shot up 172.8%. The S&P 500, by comparison, has risen only 39.7%. Lately, financial performance has been astounding. Revenue, profits, and cash flows, are all growing nicely.

In all likelihood, this trend will continue, though I am becoming increasingly concerned about the company's valuation. Although management is making great progress in boosting the tangible book value per share of the business, which is perhaps the best metric for analyzing it, at some point, a stock just becomes pricey. I wouldn't go so far as to say that investors should take a negative stance regarding SoFi Technologies. But I do think the company is priced at levels that justify a more neutral stance. Even though I fully acknowledge that I might be making this downgrade to a ‘hold’ prematurely, I would rather miss out on additional upside than to capture significant downside.

Time to slow down

From a financial standpoint, nobody will disagree with me when I say that SoFi Technologies is doing an exceptional job. Take the most recent quarter, which would be the third quarter of the 2025 fiscal year, as an example. Revenue for the quarter amounted to $961.6 million. That happens to be 37.9% above the $697.1 million the company reported a year earlier. To really understand this rise in sales, we should look at each of the core parts of the business. And what better place to start than its Financial Services segment, which has been its true powerhouse as of late?

For those not familiar, the Financial Services segment of the company includes things like its checking and savings account business that falls under SoFi Money, its mobile first investment platform known as SoFi Invest, its Loan Platform Business that offers up lending related services to its members, and more. Year over year, revenue for this unit shot up an astounding 76.1%, from $238.3 million to $419.6 million. The company benefited during this time from 16.09 million products. For context, products refers to the number of distinct offerings, like loans, accounts, and other similar offerings, that the company provides its customers. Year over year, this metric was up significantly, with that evidence by the fact that, in the third quarter of last year, total products amounted to only 11.76 million.

This wasn't the only area of growth for the company. Lending products grew from 1.89 million to 2.46 million. And in fact, according to the company, total loan originations skyrocketed during this time from $6.33 billion to $9.93 billion. That was instrumental in pushing net revenue for this segment up from $396.2 million to $493.4 million. And finally, we have the Technology Platform segment, which expanded from $102.5 million to $114.6 million. But unfortunately, management did not really provide much in the way of detail here. They did say that the number of total enabled client accounts during this time contracted from 160 million to 158 million. But that seems to be deliberate, as the company focuses on diversifying its client base for this unit. In that vein, the firm has made some progress by announcing partnerships with major U.S. consumer brands to help facilitate loyalty and engagement with their respective customers.

The overall growth that the company has achieved can be attributed to a rise in the number of users on its platform. Management calls these members. And according to the data provided, the total number of members hit 12.64 million. This was up solidly from the 10.13 million reported at the end of last year and the 7.54 million reported the year before that. As the number of members rises, the number of products must rise similarly.

As revenue for the company improved, profitability jumped as well. Net income more than doubled from $60.7 million to $139.4 million. The same trend held true when it came to adjusted net profits. Yes, we did see operating cash flow worsen from negative $1.17 billion to negative $1.31 billion. But on an adjusted basis that strips out changes in working capital, we would get an increase from $93.3 million to $240 million. Meanwhile, EBITDA for the business expanded from $186.2 million to $276.9 million.

In the chart above, you can see financial results for the first nine months of 2025 compared to the same time last year. Revenue, profits, and most cash flow metrics, or continue to grow. Pretty much any way you look at it, the picture for the business is getting better. And it is worth noting exactly why. I already touched on the number of members coming on to the platform. But that in turn has been fueled by continued innovation by management. For instance, the company continues to push for investments in AI, blockchain, and opportunities regarding cryptocurrency. It also has been focused on the launch of SoFi Pay in order to facilitate international payments utilizing blockchain. And next year, if everything goes according to plan, it intends to launch its own stablecoin backed by the US dollar. This last point, the stablecoin, is particularly intriguing to me. As I detailed in an article about payment processing giant Visa (V), this implementation of blockchain technology has great potential moving forward. So to see management move things in that direction is encouraging to say the least.

On the AI side of things, the company is making some great progress as well. Next year, for instance, the company is going to be launching SoFi Coach, which will follow its launch in the third quarter of this year of its AI-powered insights engine called Cash Coach. The goal for SoFi Coach will be to serve as an AI financial coach dedicated to all aspects of the company's financial services platform. This would include its wealth management division operations. Things like this will certainly serve to create additional shareholder value as time goes on. But even with that growth potential, we need to take valuation into consideration.

This year, management mentioned that the tangible book value of the business should grow by about $2.5 billion. Based on my own calculations, that should take it up just over $7 billion. In the chart below, you can see what this means for the valuation of the company, with a price-to-tangible book multiple of 4.79. In that same chart, I also stacked the enterprise up against five other similar firms. And what I found was that three of the five firms that have similarities to it are cheaper than it is. And honestly, the other two aren't terribly more expensive. As a value investor, I look for firms that are trading not only on the cheap on an absolute basis, but also for ones that are trading cheap relative to other comparable enterprises. And when I do this, especially with high growing growth names, my preference is to have a sufficient margin of safety involved. And I feel like that's less true today than it was in the past when I was bullish about the business.

Takeaway

Long term, I believe that the operational performance of SoFi Technologies will be fine. The company is growing nicely, and I expect that trend to continue. The innovations that management happens to be making right now should bode well for additional shareholder value creation. But as a value investor who prioritizes a good margin of safety, I don't think that I am comfortable with the stock at these prices even with these opportunities. You also need to keep in mind my own opinion that we are heading toward a recession. And while many of the technology companies will prove resilient during that time, and some might even benefit in some regard, I believe that those tied to finance are more vulnerable than those that are not. For all of these reasons, I believe that downgrading the company from a ‘buy’ to a ‘hold’ makes sense.

data.title: Calamos U.S. Opportunities Strategy Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Summary

  • Our consistent approach—maintaining conviction in AI innovation leaders and infrastructure beneficiaries while employing disciplined risk management—has delivered positive year-to-date results as key themes unfold as anticipated.
  • The economy maintains mid-cycle resilience with consumer spending growing 2.5%‒3.0% in real terms, supported by robust capital expenditure in AI infrastructure, reshoring, and broader US infrastructure reinforcement.
  • AI adoption has accelerated, with 45% of US businesses now using paid subscriptions, driving unprecedented infrastructure investment, with hyperscaler capex expectations up 30% this year alone.
  • We maintain disciplined risk management through convertibles and options strategies; we believe this is particularly important given valuations that increasingly reflect growth expectations extending well into the next decade.
  • Policy clarity continues with manageable tariff impacts and anticipated Fed easing, though structural changes in immigration and labor markets warrant monitoring.

Investment Manager Discussion

As we enter the fourth quarter of 2025, we believe our steady-course approach has continued to be validated. Rather than chasing momentum or dramatically shifting positioning, we’ve maintained focus on our core investment thesis: the transformative power of AI innovation, the critical infrastructure buildout supporting it, the resilience of quality companies, and the value of multi-asset diversification.

We believe this consistency, combined with selective adjustments, has been an important contributor to positive year-to-date performance. Calamos US Opportunities Strategy has gained 15.27% year-to-date through September 30, 2025, surpassing the Morningstar Moderately Aggressive Allocation Category return of 11.08%, as well as the S&P 500 Index return of 14.83%.

Economic Foundation Remains Solid

The US economy continues to demonstrate resilience despite evolving dynamics. Consumer spending maintains momentum at 2.5%‒3.0% real growth, primarily driven by higher-income households, which account for over 40% of total consumption and continue to benefit from wealth effects.

Meanwhile, the infrastructure supercycle continues to accelerate across multiple dimensions. Capital spending remains robust, with hyperscaler capex expectations up over 30% since January, reflecting the massive buildout of AI infrastructure. Simultaneously, reshoring initiatives drive the construction of manufacturing facilities, while federal infrastructure programs support transportation, energy grid modernization, and broadband expansion. This multi-year investment cycle creates opportunities across industrials, materials, and engineering firms while supporting sustained economic growth.

The labor market remains fundamentally healthy despite a moderation in job growth. Immigration policy shifts—with net immigration dropping from 3 million annually in 2022‒2024 to near zero— represent a significant structural change that could tighten labor markets and support wage growth, particularly for construction and infrastructure-related jobs.

AI Transformation: Accelerating Value Creation

The artificial intelligence revolution has entered the deployment phase with clear value creation emerging. Businesses with high AI exposure are rapidly adopting these tools—45% of US businesses now have paid AI subscriptions, predominantly for productivity and coding applications.

AI’s impacts span the Calamos US Opportunities Strategy portfolio through enhanced productivity in enterprise applications, emerging direct revenue streams, and margin expansion through AI-optimized operations. Critically, this transformation drives unprecedented infrastructure demand—not just for semiconductors and data centers, but also for power generation, cooling systems, and the broader ecosystem of companies supporting this buildout. While depreciation from massive infrastructure investments will partially offset margin gains, we focus on companies effectively monetizing AI as well as those providing essential infrastructure components.

Consumer Divergence Requires Selectivity

The consumer presents a nuanced picture. Top-quintile earners show the strongest wage growth, reversing post-pandemic compression trends. Combined with wealth appreciation, this cohort drives spending momentum.

Lower-income consumers face headwinds from potential tariff impacts, reduced social support, and restarting student loans, although stronger-than-usual balance sheets provide some cushion. The net impact appears manageable but requires monitoring.

Looking Forward with Disciplined Optimism

We believe our steady course has proven its worth through multiple market environments this year. We do not chase momentum; rather, our consistent focus on AI innovation leaders and high-quality companies, through our multi-asset approach, has delivered solid results. The AI revolution remains in its early stages, and companies that successfully navigate this transformation should capture disproportionate value creation.

We maintain our constructive but risk-aware stance. Current valuations increasingly reflect growth expectations extending well into the next decade, demanding continued discipline, but we see no reason to abandon proven themes. Our multi-asset structure, which combines growth equity exposure with convertibles and options overlays, provides flexibility to capture opportunities while managing downside risk. We believe this balanced approach positions Calamos US Opportunities Strategy to navigate both near-term volatility and long-term opportunities as the investment landscape continues to evolve.

John Hillenbrand, CPA

Co-CIO and Senior Co-Portfolio Manager

Market Environment

The US equity markets continued to rebound from their April lows and generated strong gains in the third quarter, demonstrating remarkable resilience in the face of persistent uncertainties. While growth sectors led the recovery, gains were broad-based, as concerns about tariffs and economic conditions faded.

The S&P 500 Index rose 8.12% for the quarter. It was another quarter in which growth beat value with the S&P 500 Growth Index up 8.90% and the S&P 500 Value gaining 6.20%, a closer spread than last quarter.

Within the S&P 500 Index, information technology (+13.2%), communication services (+12.0%), and consumer discretionary (+9.5%) outperformed the overall index. Utilities (+7.6%), energy (+6.2%), industrials (+5.0%), health care (+3.8%), financials (+3.2%), materials (+3.1%), and real estate (+0.5%) lagged with positive results, while consumer staples (-2.4%) trailed in a risk-on quarter.

Performance Review

For the quarter ended September 30, 2025, the portfolio returned 8.19% (gross of fees) and 7.99% (net of fees) versus the S&P 500 Index return of 8.12%.

Positive Influences on Performance

Industrials. The portfolio benefited from favorable security selection in industrials. Positions in the heavy electrical equipment industry and the research & consulting services industry notably contributed to relative performance.

Information Technology. Security selection in information technology, specifically in the technology hardware, storage & peripherals and communications equipment industries, contributed to performance.

Negative Influences on Performance

Consumer Discretionary. Security selection within the consumer discretionary sector weakened return. Specifically, positions in the restaurants industry and the automobile manufacturers industry lagged.

Communication Services. Security selection within the movies & entertainment and interactive media & services industries of the communication services sector lagged.

Positioning and Portfolio Changes

Our investment approach emphasizes:

  • Technology leadership—companies with clear AI monetization paths, including hyperscalers and software companies, seeing rapid AI-enhanced product adoption
  • Infrastructure beneficiaries— firms positioned to benefit from the multi-year infrastructure supercycle, including datacenter REITs, power generation, industrial equipment manufacturers, and engineering companies supporting both AI and traditional infrastructure buildout
  • Quality growth— businesses exposed to affluent consumers and those benefiting from secular trends, including housing demand
  • Financial strength—banks benefiting from regulatory normalization and yield curve dynamics, particularly those with exposure to infrastructure project financing
  • Risk management—convertible securities providing equity participation with downside protection, while options strategies add income and volatility management

Risk Management in Focus

We acknowledge key risks requiring careful monitoring:

  • Valuation extremes with market pricing increasingly dependent on growth materializing in 2027 and beyond, while momentum readings echo late-1999
  • Execution gaps between AI investment and monetization
  • Labor market uncertainty from AI automation and immigration changes
  • External factors including trade relationships and credit conditions

Regarding sectors, the largest allocations reside in information technology and financials on an absolute basis, whereas real estate and materials represent the smallest sector allocations with holdings. The portfolio takes an overweight stance in utilities and industrials versus the index, with electric utilities (in utilities) and aerospace & defense (in industrials) comprising the largest industry overweight positions. The portfolio maintains relative underweight positions in health care and information technology, with pharmaceuticals (health care) and semiconductors (information technology) comprising industry underweight positions.

The information technology and health care allocations increased during the period, with increased weights in technology hardware, storage & peripherals and health care technology. Both consumer staples and consumer discretionary allocations decreased modestly with decreased weights in consumer staples merchandise retail and broadline retail.

For additional information, please visit the strategy’s profile page:

https://www.calamos.com/strategies/us-opportunities/

Past performance does not guarantee or indicate future results. Current performance may be lower or higher than the performance quoted. Portfolios are managed according to their respective strategies which may differ significantly in terms of security holdings, industry weightings and asset allocation from those of the benchmark(s). Portfolio performance, characteristics and volatility may differ from the benchmark(s) shown.

For Institutional Use Only

The information portrayed is for the Calamos US Opportunities Strategy. Representative holdings and portfolio characteristics are specific only to the portfolio shown at that point in time. Other portfolios will vary in composition, characteristics, and will experience different investment results. The representative portfolio shown has been selected by the advisor based on account characteristics that the advisor feels accurately represents the investment strategy as a whole.

The opinions referenced are as of the date of publication and are subject to change due to changes in the market or economic conditions and may not necessarily come to pass. Information contained herein is for informational purposes only and should not be considered investment advice.

The information provided in this report should not be considered a recommendation to purchase or sell any industry, sector or particular security. There is no assurance that any industry, sector or security discussed herein will remain in a client’s account at the time of reading this report or that industry, sectors or securities sold have not been repurchased. The industries, sectors, or securities discussed herein do not represent a client’s entire account and in the aggregate may represent only a small percentage of an account’s holdings.

It should not be assumed that any of the securities transactions or holdings discussed were or will prove to be profitable, or that the investment recommendations or decisions we make in the future will be profitable or will equal the investment performance of the securities discussed herein.

Returns and Risk/Reward statistics presented reflect the Calamos US Opportunities Composite, which is an actively managed Composite that invests in equity, convertible and fixed-income securities seeking long-term total return through growth and current income. The Composite was created August 31, 2013, calculated with an inception date of January 1, 1991. The Composite results include all fully discretionary accounts, including those no longer with the Firm.

Convertible Securities Risk: The value of a convertible security is influenced by changes in interest rates, with investment value declining as interest rates increase and increasing as interest rates decline. The credit standing of the issuer and other factors also may have an effect on the convertible security’s investment value.

Equity Securities Risk: The securities markets are volatile, and the market prices of the securities may decline generally. The price of equity securities fluctuates based on changes in a company’s financial condition and overall market and economic conditions. If the market prices of the securities owned fall, the value of your investment will decline.

The ICE BofA All US Convertibles Index (VXA0) is comprised of approximately 700 issues of only convertible bonds and preferreds of all qualities and measures the return of all US convertibles. The ICE BofA All US Convertibles Ex Mandatory Index(V0A0) represents the US convertible market excluding mandatory convertibles. Source ICE Data Indices, LLC, used with permission. ICE permits use of the ICE BofA indices and related data on an ‘as is’ basis, makes no warranties regarding same, does not guarantee the suitability, quality, accuracy, timeliness, and/or completeness of the ICE BofA Indices or data included in, related to, or derived therefrom, assumes no liability in connection with the use of the foregoing and does not sponsor, endorse or recommend Calamos Advisors LLC or any of its products or services. The Cboe Volatility Index or VIX (based on its Cboe ticker symbol) shows the market’s expectation of 30-day volatility. It is constructed using the implied volatilities of a wide range of S&P 500 Index options. The Russell 3000 Growth Index measures the performance of those Russell 3000 Index companies with higher price-to-book ratios and higher forecasted growth values. The stocks in this index are also members of either the Russell 1000 or the Russell 2000 Growth Indexes. The Russell 3000 Value Index measures the performance of those companies in the Russell 3000 Index with lower price-to-book ratios and lower forecasted growth values. The S&P 500 Index is a market value weighted index consisting of 500 stocks chosen for market size, liquidity, and industry group representation. It is widely regarded as the standard for measuring US stock market performance. The S&P 500 Growth Index measures the performance of stocks within the S&P 500 Index that have growth-oriented characteristics, such as higher earnings growth rates and higher price-to-earnings ratios. The S&P 500 Value Index measures the performance of stocks within the S&P 500 Index that have value-oriented characteristics, such as lower price-to-earnings ratios and price-to-book ratios. Unmanaged index returns assume reinvestment of any and all distributions and do not reflect any fees, expenses or sales charges. Investors cannot invest directly in an index.

Fees include the investment advisory fee charged by Calamos Advisors LLC. Returns greater than 12 months are annualized. Chart Data Source: Calamos Advisors LLC.

Average annual total return measures net investment income and capital gain or loss from portfolio investments as an annualized average assuming reinvestment of dividends and capital gains distributions.
